description: The component-workbench / docs-tool landscape — the consolidated inventory of Storybook-like tools and the formats they read and emit (backlog #624, feeder for the Web Docs pipeline epic #623). Until now only Storybook + Mintlify were named (as ingest targets in #426/#429); this registry enumerates the whole landscape so the feature-surface sweep (#625) and the derive-to-WE-standards decision (#626) have a complete, reproducible source list. Membership is governed by the selectionCriteria below so a re-run lands on a comparable list. The benchmarkCorpus.json (design systems) and this file (the tools that DOCUMENT design systems) are complementary corpora: one is what Web Docs would render, the other is what Web Docs competes with and ingests from.

selectionCriteria.label: Occupies a workbench/docs axis
selectionCriteria.test: Is a tool whose primary job is rendering, documenting, testing, or publishing UI components or a design system — not a general site generator or a component library itself.

selectionCriteria.label: Adoption / influence
selectionCriteria.test: Widely used, widely copied, or sets a pattern other tools follow (a reference point, not a niche one-off).

selectionCriteria.label: Distinct model or philosophy
selectionCriteria.test: Adds a perspective the corpus would otherwise miss (local vs hosted, code-first vs design-first, OSS vs commercial, framework-coupled vs agnostic).

selectionCriteria.label: Format relevance
selectionCriteria.test: Reads or emits a machine-readable component format (CSF, CEM, web-types, react-docgen, MDX) that Web Docs would either ingest from or emit.

selectionCriteria.label: Maintenance / currency
selectionCriteria.test: Actively maintained, OR notable enough that its retirement/decline is itself a signal worth recording.

inclusionRule: A tool qualifies if it occupies one of the category axes below AND scores on at least two other criteria. The corpus deliberately spans all five axes (local workbench, docs platform, hosted catalog, visual-test/publish, inventory format) rather than over-weighting Storybook's orbit, so the feature sweep is not biased toward one model. Retired/retiring tools are kept (flagged via status) because their decline is evidence about which models endure.

categories.label: Local component workbench
categories.axis: Runs in the project repo; renders components in isolation from source/stories. Code-first, OSS, developer-owned (Storybook's lineage).

categories.label: Docs platform / site generator
categories.axis: Authors long-form documentation sites (often MDX/Markdown) with component examples interleaved; not component-isolation-first.

categories.label: Hosted design-system catalog
categories.axis: SaaS that hosts a browsable design-system catalog, often bridging design (Figma) and code; commercial, design-org-owned.

categories.label: Visual-test / publish service
categories.axis: Snapshots and diffs rendered components (usually driving a local workbench) and publishes review/approval workflows.

categories.label: Machine-readable inventory format
categories.axis: A schema/standard describing a component's API (props, slots, events, examples) — both an ingest source AND a format Web Docs should emit. Not a tool but a contract tools agree on.

sources.name: Storybook
notableFeatures: Storybook Test (Vitest): interaction + a11y + visual + coverage from one widget (v9)
sources.notes: The category-defining workbench and the named primary ingest target (#426). v9 (Jun 2025) folded testing/a11y/visual into core. CSF3 is its de-facto story format.

sources.name: Storybook for Web Components
notableFeatures: Autodocs generated from Custom Elements Manifest
sources.notes: The Storybook renderer most relevant to WE — it is custom-element-native and consumes CEM, the format WE's web-component-aligned blocks would emit.

sources.name: Histoire
sources.notes: Vite-native Storybook alternative. Pre-v1, small (effectively one-maintainer) team — a sustainability caution, but the Vite-native model and component-as-story authoring are donor ideas.

sources.name: Ladle
notableFeatures: Vite-based, drop-in CSF compatibility with Storybook
sources.notes: Reads Storybook CSF directly — proves CSF is a portable contract, not a Storybook-internal format. Relevant to whether WE should treat CSF as an interchange format.

sources.name: React Styleguidist
notableFeatures: Examples authored in Markdown next to components
sources.notes: An early autodocs-from-types model (react-docgen prop tables). Largely superseded by Storybook autodocs; low recent activity. Kept as the origin of the 'docs from component source' pattern.

sources.name: Pattern Lab
sources.notes: Predates the JS-component era; the canonical Atomic Design workbench. Its taxonomy (the IA of a component catalog) is the donor idea, not its template engine.

sources.name: Docusaurus
sources.notes: The dominant OSS long-form docs platform. Versioning + i18n + search are the docs-platform features a WE-native surface must answer.

sources.name: Mintlify
notableFeatures: Hosted MDX docs with polished defaults
sources.notes: The second named ingest target (#426). Represents the modern hosted-docs model (managed, AI-augmented) versus Docusaurus's self-hosted OSS model.

sources.name: Nextra
sources.notes: Next.js-native docs platform. Confirms MDX-in-React as the prevailing docs authoring substrate.

sources.name: Starlight
sources.notes: The native-first donor: ships near-zero JS by default and is framework-agnostic — closest in philosophy to a WE-native (web-component, minimal-runtime) docs surface.

sources.name: GitBook
sources.notes: Hosted docs aimed at mixed (non-developer) authoring teams. Donor signal: WYSIWYG authoring as a peer to code-first.

sources.name: ReadMe
sources.notes: API-reference-centric hosted docs. Its metered/personalized model is relevant to Web Docs open-core tiering (#398).

sources.name: Zeroheight
sources.notes: The design-org's catalog: documents a design system for designers + stakeholders, embedding code tools rather than replacing them. Donor: the design-to-code bridge as a first-class concern.

sources.name: Supernova
sources.notes: Token-pipeline-centric catalog: imports design tokens and emits platform code. Donor: token tables + multi-target token export (relevant to #625's design-token-tables axis).

sources.name: Knapsack
notableFeatures: Combines docs + live components + tokens + guidelines
sources.notes: Enterprise design-system platform bundling catalog + docs + governance. Represents the 'full managed design-system home' end of the spectrum.

sources.name: Bit (bit.dev)
sources.notes: Catalog model built around independently versioned components. Donor: per-component versioning + composition rendering as a publishing primitive.

sources.name: Backlight
sources.notes: RETIRED — shut down 1 Jun 2025. Kept as evidence: the fully-hosted 'design-system IDE in the browser' model did not endure, a signal for #626 about where WE should NOT invest.

sources.name: Chromatic
sources.notes: The canonical Storybook companion: turns stories into a visual-test + publish workflow. Donor: snapshot/diff + approval as a catalog-publishing step.

sources.name: Percy
sources.notes: Framework-agnostic visual testing (not Storybook-coupled). Represents visual-test as a CI service independent of the workbench.

sources.name: Lost Pixel
sources.notes: The OSS, self-hostable visual-test option — the open-core counterpart to Chromatic/Percy. Multi-workbench by design. Donor: visual-test as an OSS capability layered onto any renderer.

sources.name: Applitools
sources.notes: Enterprise visual-test with AI diffing. Represents the high-end commercial ceiling of the visual-test axis.

sources.name: Custom Elements Manifest (CEM)
notableFeatures: JSON schema describing custom elements: tags, attributes, properties, slots, events, CSS parts/vars
Consumed by Storybook, api-viewer, IDEs (VS Code via web-types bridge)
sources.notes: THE format for WE: web-component-native, the inventory standard WE's blocks should EMIT and tools should ingest. The strongest format-to-emit candidate for the Web Docs surface.

sources.name: Component Story Format (CSF3)
notableFeatures: Portable across Storybook, Ladle, testing libraries
sources.notes: The de-facto story interchange format (Ladle + testing tools read it too). An ingest source for examples/states; less natural for WE to EMIT than CEM since it is JS-module, not declarative JSON.

sources.name: api-viewer
notableFeatures: Web component (<api-viewer>) that renders CEM as live docs + props playground
sources.notes: Proof that CEM alone can drive an interactive docs+playground surface with no build step — the closest existing analog to a WE-native (web-component, CEM-driven) docs element.

sources.name: web-types
notableFeatures: JSON describing framework/web components for IDE autocomplete
Consumed by JetBrains IDEs (and bridgeable to VS Code)
sources.notes: The IDE-tooling inventory format (JetBrains). Overlaps CEM in intent; relevant if Web Docs wants editor-integration parity, but CEM is the more standards-aligned emit target.

sources.name: react-docgen
notableFeatures: Extracts prop types + descriptions from React component source
sources.notes: The dominant React autodocs source. Included as the framework-coupled counterpoint to CEM — most existing autodocs are react-docgen-driven, an ingest surface, but NOT a format WE would emit (WE is web-component-native).
